rationale: Both articles cover the same Rightmove class-action lawsuit. The 'new participant' article is a development update of the story first reported in c1 — same legal case (Hausfeld-managed), same defendants and plaintiffs. The dollar amount differs (£1.5B vs $2B) only due to currency conversion and a new claimant joining. This tests whether LLM can recognise the same event despite different numerical framing.
benchmark_note: KEY CONFIRMED DUPLICATE from project dedupe.json — story_id matches. Both articles share story_id 'story_rightmove_agent_lawsuit_2026'.

rationale: Zillow AI Mode (new article) and CoStar AI on Homes.com (c1) + Realtor.com AI-first (c2) are all about AI search, but they are distinct events at different companies. These are thematically related signals — industry-level trend — but NOT duplicates. Each represents a separate product launch at a separate company that should be tracked independently.
benchmark_note: CRITICAL FALSE POSITIVE TEST. LLM must not conflate 'same topic' with 'same event'. Multiple portal AI launches in the same period are separate signals.

rationale: The new article ('Zillow Preview gains momentum') is a follow-up on the Zillow Preview product, and c1 is the original launch announcement. These are the same product but DIFFERENT events — launch vs. adoption traction — separated by 8 days. They share a story_id in the system ('story_zillow_preview_202603') but the story_id groups related events, not just duplicates. c2 (Homes.com pre-market expansion) is a similar product at a competitor — related but definitely not a duplicate.
benchmark_note: This tests the distinction between story_id 'grouping' and actual deduplication. story_id clusters related events; is_duplicate only when the same underlying event is reported by multiple sources.

rationale: The new article (amended CoStar complaint against Zillow) directly continues the same legal case as c1 (original CoStar IP suit filed October 2025). c2 and c3 are unrelated lawsuits (different parties, different subject matter). The amended complaint is the same legal proceeding with expanded claims — not a new event, but an update to an ongoing case. Should be flagged as a duplicate of the original filing.
benchmark_note: Tests whether LLM can recognise case updates as duplicates of the original event. The amended complaint is NOT a new lawsuit — it's an update to an existing one filed months earlier.

rationale: The new article is a Zillow thought leadership piece about their AI/Zestimate history — editorial content, not a product news announcement. c1 (Zillow AI Mode search launch) and c3 (AI search adoption data) are related topics but completely different events. This is background narrative content, not news of a new event.
benchmark_note: Tests whether LLM correctly handles editorial/analysis pieces as non-duplicates vs. product news articles about the same company.

rationale: The new article ('Ask Realtor' launch from Realtor.com's own newsroom) and c1 (Inman's coverage of the same launch) describe the identical product launch event. The same product, same day, different sources. This is a classic cross-source semantic duplicate — different headline ('Ask Realtor launch' vs 'AI-first search'), different framing (product announcement vs industry analysis), but the same underlying event. c2 and c3 are unrelated events.
benchmark_note: CORE SEMANTIC DUPLICATE TEST. Same event, different headline style (company newsroom vs. trade publication), different framing. LLM must recognise these as the same event despite no textual similarity in headlines.

rationale: OpenSooq's strategy piece (new article) covers vertical depth-building at a MENA horizontal classifieds platform. c1 (Dubizzle full transaction platform) is a different company making a different product move in overlapping geography. These are industry peers making related but distinct strategic moves — thematically linked but separate events. Not duplicates.
benchmark_note: Tests peer company thematic clustering — two MENA classifieds companies pursuing similar strategies should NOT be flagged as duplicates.

rationale: The new article is a detailed technical deep dive on Realtor.com's AI search product (April 6) following the original launch coverage in c1 (April 4). c2 is a third article from Realtor.com's own newsroom about the same launch. The deep dive adds technical detail (architecture, screenshots) but covers the same product launch event. In context of a monitoring system that has already included c1 in the digest, this is a duplicate.
benchmark_note: Tests follow-up/analysis coverage. A 'deep dive' article published 2 days after the launch announcement about the same product is a semantic duplicate. The monitoring system should surface the original, not both.

rationale: The new article is the deal completion announcement (September 2026) for the Scout24 acquisition that was originally announced in March 2026 (c1). These are the SAME deal but different phases — announcement vs. completion — separated by 6 months. In a monitoring system, the completion of an already-tracked deal should ideally be flagged as 'same story, new development' rather than a new event. However, 6 months later with new operational detail (integration begins, day-one quote) means it has meaningful new content. Edge case: acceptable to flag as either duplicate or new event with a 'continuation' note.
benchmark_note: EDGE CASE: M&A announcement vs. completion 6 months later. Both 'is_duplicate: true' and 'is_duplicate: false' with proper rationale are acceptable.

rationale: The new article (Russian-language RBC overview of Avito real estate) references Avito products but is a general market overview article — not the same event as c1 (Avito Adtech analytics launch) or c2 (Avito Jobs branded format). The new article is meta-commentary about Avito as a platform, not a specific product announcement. Different topic, different purpose. Not a duplicate.
benchmark_note: Tests cross-language and editorial-genre boundary. A market analysis article referencing a company is NOT a duplicate of the company's product news.

rationale: Zillow rental management product (new article) and AI rental application process article (c1) are both about rentals and AI but represent completely different product categories and companies. The new article is about landlord lease management tooling by Zillow; c1 is about tenant screening AI from third-party startups. No overlap in event, company, or product.
benchmark_note: Tests intra-topic false positive. Two 'rental + AI' articles that are clearly different events.

rationale: The new article covers Avito's own AI search launch (Russian market, April 2026). c1 (Realtor.com AI search) and c2 (Zillow AI search) are related thematically as international peers, but these are events at completely different companies in different markets. The Avito launch is a NEW, DISTINCT, HIGH-PRIORITY event that must NOT be suppressed as a duplicate of the international coverage. This is a critical false-positive risk scenario.
benchmark_note: CRITICAL FALSE POSITIVE: LLM must NOT flag Avito's own AI search launch as a duplicate of Zillow or Realtor.com AI search coverage. These are peer events, not the same event. The Avito launch is independent and should be treated as a primary event.

rationale: The new article is Rightmove's Q1 trading update (financial results). c1 and c2 are about the ongoing lawsuit against Rightmove, and c3 is about Rightmove's new subscription model. These are all Rightmove news but completely different events — financial results vs. legal proceedings vs. pricing changes. None are duplicates despite being about the same company.
benchmark_note: Tests same-company clustering. Four different Rightmove articles in corpus — none are duplicates of the trading update.

rationale: The new article (Lofty $30M Series B funding) and c1 (Lofty AI CRM product launch) are about the same company but different events — product launch (April 3) vs. fundraising round (April 7). These are distinct business events that should both be tracked. The product launch is about what the company built; the fundraise is about its capital structure and growth plans.
benchmark_note: Same company, different event types. Product launch ≠ fundraise even at the same company.

rationale: The new article (Novostroy.ru Russian-language analysis of Zillow Preview) is a secondary analysis piece that derives entirely from c1 (original Zillow Preview press release) and cites it as its source. While it adds Russian-market commentary, the primary factual content is from c1. For deduplication purposes, this should be flagged as a duplicate of c1 — the Zillow Preview launch event has already been captured in the corpus. The secondary analysis adds interpretive value but not a new event.
benchmark_note: Tests secondary/derived content detection. Russian-language analysis of a US portal launch should be flagged as a duplicate of the original — the event is the same.
